article_title: Massachusetts Becomes First State High Court to Rule Section 230 Does Not Shield Social Media Design Choices
summary: Substantively important ruling, mostly well-sourced — Volokh (source-3) verifies the legal analysis, the Justice Wendlandt authorship, the two-part test, and the central court quotes about features inducing compulsive use; CNBC (source-2) verifies AG Andrea Joy Campbell, the April 10 date, and the under-13 age rule; EPIC (source-1) verifies the amicus brief and complaint structure; Techdirt (source-4) verifies the 'thousand workarounds' line. BUT two material problems: (1) the article quotes CNBC for 'a full panel of justices at the highest level in a state has agreed that the 1996 federal law does not shield against complaints about specific features baked into an app or platform' — that exact phrasing is NOT in source-2; CNBC actually says 'first time a state high court has considered whether a federal law...'. The quoted CNBC phrasing appears fabricated; (2) the compound court quote 'do not seek to impose liability on Meta for information provided by third parties... allege harm stemming from Meta's own conduct either by designing a social media platform that capitalizes on the developmental vulnerabilities of children or by affirmatively misleading consumers about the safety of the Instagram platform' splices verifiable Volokh language with a trailing fragment ('or by affirmatively misleading consumers about the safety of the Instagram platform') that is NOT in source-3. Plus the '2025' year for the trial court ruling is not in any readable source.

findings.message: Source fetched via Archive.org fallback (original returned 200)
Fabricated direct quote attributed to CNBC
findings.details: Article: CNBC characterized the ruling as the first time 'a full panel of justices at the highest level in a state has agreed that the 1996 federal law does not shield against complaints about specific features baked into an app or platform'. Source-2 (CNBC) does NOT contain this phrasing. CNBC's actual phrasing is along the lines of 'first time a state high court has considered whether a federal law...'. Replace with the verbatim CNBC phrasing or recast as paraphrase without quotation marks.

findings.message: Compound court quote splices unverifiable trailing fragment
findings.details: Article quotes the court as saying claims 'allege harm stemming from Meta's own conduct either by designing a social media platform that capitalizes on the developmental vulnerabilities of children or by affirmatively misleading consumers about the safety of the Instagram platform.' Source-3 (Volokh) contains the first half but its excerpt ENDS at 'vulnerabilities of children'; the 'or by affirmatively misleading consumers about the safety of the Instagram platform' tail is not in any readable source. The full opinion is on Justia (source-0) but bot-blocked. Either split the quote, replace with the Volokh-verified portion only, or pull the missing fragment from a non-blocked source that quotes the opinion directly.

findings.message: 'A trial court rejected that argument in 2025' — date not in any readable source
findings.details: Source-2 (CNBC) confirms a trial-court judge agreed Meta could not invoke 230, but no readable source states the year as 2025. Drop the year or source it separately.

findings.message: Docket number SJC-13747 inferred from blocked Justia URL
findings.details: The 'SJC-13747' docket number appears only in the Justia URL slug (source-0, 403 bot-blocked). It is consistent with the URL but not independently verifiable through readable sources. Acceptable as a URL-derived inference but should be flagged or replaced with a non-blocked source citation.

findings.message: Most claims verify cleanly across the readable sources
findings.details: Verified: April 10, 2026 decision date (source-1, source-2, source-3); Justice Dalila Argaez Wendlandt authoring (source-3); two-part test with dissemination + content elements (source-3); 'the published information itself is not the source of the harm alleged. Instead, the claim alleges that the features themselves induce compulsive use' verbatim (source-3, also source-4); AG Andrea Joy Campbell (source-2); causes of action — unfair + deceptive + public nuisance (source-3, source-4); features paraphrased — infinite scrolling, autoplay, intermittent variable rewards, notifications (source-3, source-4, source-2); under-13 minimum age (source-2); EPIC amicus filing (source-1); Techdirt 'Section 230 is dying by a thousand workarounds, and Massachusetts just added another one' verbatim from source-4 headline; SJC ruling is procedural at motion-to-dismiss stage, not on merits (source-3, source-4); case caption 'Commonwealth v. Meta Platforms, Inc.' (source-3, source-4).

content_preview.title: Massachusetts Becomes First State High Court to Rule Section 230 Does Not Shield Social Media Design Choices
content_preview.summary: The Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court ruled April 10 that Section 230 does not bar Attorney General Andrea Campbell's lawsuit against Meta over Instagram's addictive design, the first such ruling by any state's highest court.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court ruled on April 10, 2026 that Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act does not immunize Meta Platforms from claims that Instagram's addictive design features harm children, allowing Attorney General Andrea Campbell's consumer protection lawsuit to proceed past the motion-to-dismiss stage. The decision in *Commonwealth v. Meta Platforms, Inc.* (SJC-13747) is the first time any state's highest court has held that the 1996 federal liability...

recommendations: Replace the fabricated CNBC quote with verbatim CNBC phrasing or convert to paraphrase without quote marks.
Split the compound court quote: keep the Volokh-verified portion and pull the trailing 'or by affirmatively misleading...' fragment from a readable source or drop it.
Drop the '2025' year for the trial-court ruling (or source it separately).
Consider citing a non-blocked source for the docket number, or note that the docket number is inferred from the Justia URL.
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Substantively important Policy & Regulation News piece (~1090 words; under the 1200-word News ceiling). Clear structure (Overview / What the Court Decided / The Underlying Lawsuit / Why the Section 230 Argument Failed / A First-of-Its-Kind Ruling / Connection to Earlier Verdicts / What We Don't Know), neutral tone, strong legal-doctrinal framing.
editor_notes.source_verification: Read source-1 (EPIC via Archive.org), source-2 (CNBC), source-3 (Volokh / Reason), and source-4 (Techdirt) in full. Source-0 (Justia opinion text) is 403 bot-blocked. Most of the article's substance verifies cleanly across the readable sources: the April 10 decision date, AG Campbell, Justice Wendlandt authoring, the two-part test, the central feature-vs-content quote ('the published information itself is not the source of the harm alleged. Instead, the claim alleges that the features themselves induce compulsive use'), the causes of action, the named features, the under-13 age requirement, the EPIC amicus, the Techdirt 'thousand workarounds' line, the procedural-not-merits framing, and the case caption. Two material problems: (a) the article presents 'a full panel of justices at the highest level in a state has agreed that the 1996 federal law does not shield against complaints about specific features baked into an app or platform' as a direct CNBC quote, but that exact phrasing is NOT in source-2 — this is a fabricated direct quote attributed to a named outlet; (b) the article splices the verifiable Volokh court quote with a trailing fragment ('or by affirmatively misleading consumers about the safety of the Instagram platform') that is NOT in source-3 and could only have come from the bot-blocked Justia opinion. Plus 'a trial court rejected that argument in 2025' — '2025' is not in any readable source.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: The legal substance is sound and the broad facts are well-cross-referenced. But two fabricated quotation passages — one attributed to CNBC, one to the court — are serious editorial defects regardless of how plausible the underlying meaning is. Direct quotes inside quotation marks must be verbatim from a readable source.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and well-structured. The 'engineered behavior of their products rather than for the speech those products carry' framing is appropriately analytical without editorializing.
editor_notes.originality: New ruling on April 10, no overlap with prior coverage. Internal cross-reference to the Meta/Google jury-verdicts article is appropriate context.
concerns: Fabricated direct quote attributed to CNBC.
Compound court quote includes a trailing fragment not in any readable source.
'2025' trial-court date is not in any readable source.
Docket number derived only from blocked Justia URL.
recommendations: Five fixes per findings — the two fabricated-quote items are the most serious.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Otherwise a well-reported and substantively important piece. Direct quotes must be verbatim from readable sources; fix the two fabricated-quote issues and the year, and this is cleanly approvable.
